assumptions: The question is about hedge designation in consolidated financial statements.
The intragroup dividend balance is a receivable/payable exposed to foreign currency movements.
If the entities involved do not have different functional currencies, there is no qualifying foreign currency exposure to hedge.

approaches.label: General prohibition for intragroup hedged items in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items must generally involve an external party; intragroup items are excluded as a general rule.
approaches.practical_implication: The intragroup dividend receivable cannot be designated unless a specific IFRS exception applies.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 limits hedged items in consolidation to exposures with parties external to the reporting entity.

approaches.label: Foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 expressly allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item, such as a receivable/payable, to qualify in consolidated financial statements if the FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: The dividend receivable/payable is an intragroup monetary item.
The counterparties have different functional currencies.
The FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
Formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness requirements are met.
approaches.practical_implication: The FX exposure on the receivable may be designated as the hedged item despite being intragroup.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits foreign currency risk on an intragroup monetary item in consolidated financial statements.
Paragraphs 6.3.2 and 6.4.1 require reliable measurement plus formal designation, documentation and effectiveness.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of a forecast intragroup transaction
approaches.reasoning: This route applies only to highly probable forecast intragroup transactions whose foreign currency risk affects consolidated profit or loss; a recognised dividend receivable is not a forecast transaction.
approaches.practical_implication: The forecast-transaction exception does not support hedge designation of the already recognised dividend receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows certain forecast intragroup transactions only when foreign currency risk affects consolidated profit or loss.
Paragraph B6.3.5 explains that only intragroup transactions affecting consolidated profit or loss can qualify.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge as a separate alternative basis
approaches.reasoning: A hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation is a distinct IFRS model: the hedged item is an amount of net assets, not simply any intragroup receivable, and the interpretation cannot be applied by analogy to other hedges.
conditions: The hedged item is an amount of net assets of a foreign operation included in consolidated financial statements.
The designated risk is foreign currency risk between relevant functional currencies.
IFRS 9 designation, documentation and effectiveness requirements are satisfied.
approaches.practical_implication: This is relevant only if the entity structures the hedge as a net investment hedge instead of hedging the dividend receivable itself.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 10–14 permit hedging foreign currency risk of a net investment in a foreign operation and define the hedged item as net assets.
Paragraph 8 says the interpretation applies only to net investment hedges and should not be applied by analogy to other hedge relationships.
Paragraph 6.4.1 sets the designation, documentation and effectiveness criteria that also apply to this hedge model.
